title: Aggregation Pipeline
description: Transform, group, and join data using MongoDB's powerful aggregation pipeline — the equivalent of SQL GROUP BY, HAVING, and JOIN.

sections.content: The aggregation pipeline is a sequence of **stages** — each stage transforms the documents and passes results to the next. Think of it as a Unix pipe: `query | filter | group | sort | project`.

sections.heading: Your first pipeline
sections.content:
// SQL: SELECT genres, COUNT(*) FROM books WHERE inStock = true GROUP BY genres
db.books.aggregate([
  { $match:   { inStock: true } },            // WHERE clause
  { $unwind:  '$genres' },                    // flatten the genres array
  { $group:   { _id: '$genres', count: { $sum: 1 } } },  // GROUP BY + COUNT
  { $sort:    { count: -1 } },                // ORDER BY count DESC
  { $limit:   5 }                             // LIMIT 5
])

sections.heading: Aggregation accumulators
$match after $group (HAVING equivalent)

sections.content: `$lookup` is MongoDB's version of a SQL JOIN. It lets you combine documents from two collections.

sections.heading: $lookup — basic join (like LEFT JOIN)

sections.content: **$lookup returns an array** even when there's only one match. Use `$unwind` immediately after to flatten it to a single document (like an INNER JOIN). Without `$unwind`, you get a LEFT JOIN that always succeeds.

sections.heading: $project — reshape documents

sections.content: **$addToSet vs $push.** Both are accumulators for `$group`. `$push` collects all values including duplicates into an array. `$addToSet` collects only unique values (like a Set).

sections.heading: $push vs $addToSet

sections.content: **$switch — conditional logic inside a pipeline.** Just like SQL's `CASE WHEN`, `$switch` lets you return different values based on conditions. Use it inside `$project` or `$addFields`.

sections.heading: $switch — equivalent to SQL CASE WHEN

sections.content: **$facet — multiple pipelines on the same data at once.** `$facet` runs several sub-pipelines on the same input documents in parallel, returning all results in one response. Great for search result pages that need counts by category, price ranges, and top results all at once.

sections.heading: $facet — run multiple aggregations in one query

sections.content: **$out — write pipeline results to a collection.** `$out` is the last stage of a pipeline that writes results to a named collection. If the collection already exists, it is completely replaced. Use with caution.

sections.heading: $out — save aggregation results as a new collection

sections.content: **Advanced $lookup with pipeline.** The basic `$lookup` joins on exact field equality. The advanced form uses `let` + `pipeline` to write any join condition — including filtering the joined collection before returning results.

sections.heading: Advanced $lookup with pipeline

sections.content: **MongoDB auto-optimises your pipeline.** The aggregation engine automatically moves `$match` and `$sort` stages earlier in the pipeline when it's safe to do so. It also merges consecutive `$limit` or `$skip` stages. Write pipelines for readability — the engine handles the optimisation.
